payload.text:
Steps: Open Safari 17.4 on macOS and navigate to the blog post editor. Enter a title containing at least one emoji character. Type some content into the body field. Click the 'Save Draft' button located in the top toolbar. Observe the state of the editor body immediately after the click action completes.

Expected: The system should save the draft data to the server without modifying the local editor state. The body text should remain visible and unchanged, allowing continued editing.

Actual: Clicking the button triggers a script error that clears the entire editor body content. The text disappears completely, forcing the user to retype their work. This regression only occurs when the title includes an emoji. Other browsers and standard titles do not reproduce this issue, indicating a specific encoding or event handling bug within Safari 17.4.

rubric.task_summary: Generate a concise bug report for Safari 17.4 with specific formatting and content constraints.
failure_modes: Omission of Safari 17.4 or emoji trigger
Use of markdown headers or filler text

candidates.solution:
Steps: Open the blog post editor in Safari 17.4 on macOS. Type a title containing at least one emoji character. Enter some text into the editor body. Click the 'Save Draft' button located in the toolbar. Observe the state of the editor body immediately after the click action completes.

Expected: The system should save the current draft to the database without modifying the content visible in the editor body. The text entered by the user must remain intact and visible.

Actual: The editor body is completely cleared of all user input. The text disappears instantly upon clicking the button. This regression occurs exclusively when the title contains an emoji. Other browsers and titles without emojis do not exhibit this behavior.
assumptions: The word count includes all text in the solution string.
Safari 17.4 is the specific version mentioned.
known_risks: Word count might be slightly off due to tokenization differences.
Emoji rendering might affect character count in some systems.

candidates.solution:
Steps: Open the blog post editor in Safari 17.4 on macOS. Enter a title that includes at least one emoji character. Type any content into the editor body field. Click the 'Save Draft' button located in the toolbar.

Expected: The system should save the current draft state, preserving both the emoji-containing title and the body text. The editor should remain populated with the entered content.

Actual: Clicking 'Save Draft' causes the editor body to be instantly cleared. The title remains visible with the emoji, but all body content disappears. This behavior is specific to Safari 17.4 and does not occur in other browsers or when the title lacks emojis.
assumptions: The user assumes 'Safari 17.4' is the specific version causing the issue.
The term 'blog post editor' refers to a standard web-based interface.
known_risks: Word count might be slightly off if tokenization differs from standard word counting.
The constraint 'no markdown headers' is strict, so plain text labels are used.

candidates.solution:
Steps: Open Safari version 17.4 on macOS. Navigate to the blog post editor interface. Compose a new post and intentionally insert an emoji character into the title field. Type any valid content into the editor body area. Click the 'Save Draft' button located in the top toolbar. Observe the state of the editor content immediately after the click event completes and any loading indicators disappear from the interface.

Expected: The system should persist the current title and body content to the database. The editor should refresh or display a success notification. The text in the body area must remain intact and visible to the user, allowing for further edits or publication.

Actual: Clicking 'Save Draft' while the title contains an emoji causes the editor body to be completely cleared. All entered text vanishes instantly. The save action fails silently without error messages. This issue reproduces consistently only in this specific browser and version combination. Standard ASCII titles do not trigger this bug, confirming it is an edge case related to Unicode handling in the save handler.
assumptions: The bug report format requires three specific labeled sections.
Word count includes all text within the solution string.
Engineering-style prose implies concise, factual language without fluff.
known_risks: Word count might exceed 120 words if sections are too verbose.
Specific version numbers like 17.4 must be exact.
Emoji mention must be explicit regarding the title trigger.

candidates.solution:
Steps: Open Safari 17.4 on macOS. Navigate to the blog post editor interface. Enter a title containing at least one emoji character. Type arbitrary text into the editor body area. Click the 'Save Draft' button located in the toolbar. Observe the state of the editor body immediately after the click action completes.

Expected: The system should persist the current draft data to the database and display a success notification. The editor body should retain all previously entered text without modification or loss.

Actual: The editor body content is completely cleared upon clicking the button. No data is saved. This regression occurs exclusively when the title includes an emoji. Other browsers and non-emoji titles do not reproduce the issue.
assumptions: The word count includes all text within the solution string.
Standard whitespace tokenization is used for word counting.
known_risks: Word count might be slightly off depending on tokenization rules.
Might be too brief for some engineering standards, but meets constraints.

candidates.solution:
Steps: Open Safari 17.4 on macOS. Navigate to the blog post editor interface. Enter a title containing at least one emoji character. Type several paragraphs of text into the editor body field. Locate and click the 'Save Draft' button. Observe the state of the editor body immediately after the click action completes.

Expected: The system should save the current content and display a success notification. The editor body should retain all previously entered text, including the title with the emoji.

Actual: Clicking the button clears the entire editor body content. The text is lost, and only the title remains visible. This regression occurs exclusively in Safari 17.4 when the title includes an emoji. Other browsers do not exhibit this behavior.
assumptions: The editor body is considered empty if all text is removed.
known_risks: Word count might be slightly off due to hyphenation or punctuation interpretation.

candidates.solution:
Steps:
Open Safari 17.4 on macOS and navigate to the blog post editor interface. Enter a unique title string that includes at least one Unicode emoji character. Type a substantial amount of text content into the main body field. Locate and click the 'Save Draft' button situated in the toolbar. Observe the behavior of the editor content immediately after the click event completes and the save operation attempts to persist data to the backend server.

Expected:
The system should process the save request, store the current body content and title, and display a success notification. The editor body must retain all entered text without any modification or loss of data during the asynchronous save process.

Actual:
Clicking the button triggers an immediate clearing of the editor body. All input text disappears from the view, and the save operation fails silently. This regression occurs exclusively when the title contains an emoji in this specific browser version, indicating a parsing error in the payload serialization logic.
assumptions: The user considers 'Safari' and 'Safari 17.4' as sufficient for the mention constraint.
The word count includes the section labels.
Emoji is considered a single word or part of the title string for counting purposes.
known_risks: Word count might exceed 120 words due to verbose descriptions.
The term 'emoji' might not be explicitly counted as a separate word by some counters.
Missing the specific version '17.4' if only 'Safari' is mentioned could be a failure, but I included both.

Actual:

payload.final_text:
Steps: Open the blog post editor in Safari 17.4 on macOS. Enter a title that includes at least one emoji character. Type any content into the editor body field. Click the 'Save Draft' button located in the toolbar.

Expected: The system should save the current draft state, preserving both the emoji-containing title and the body text. The editor should remain populated with the entered content.

Actual: Clicking 'Save Draft' causes the editor body to be instantly cleared. The title remains visible with the emoji, but all body content disappears. This behavior is specific to Safari 17.4 and does not occur in other browsers or when the title lacks emojis.
